title: Epic: Build OpenCode Village Slidev Presentation
description:
## Context

Build a ~15 minute Slidev presentation showcasing the OpenCode Village orchestration workflow. The presentation lives at ~/Repositories/slidev-village and covers: the village model (5 agents), beads issue tracking, slash commands, a success story (Omni in Slack — 22 merged Go PRs with no prior Go knowledge), drawbacks, and GitHub links/QR codes.

Target audience: mixed (engineers + PM/management). Visual style: dark theme based on AstroNvim Vivid palette (#000000 bg, #57b6ff blue, #eba0ff purple, #5efff5 cyan).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Slidev project initialised and runnable at ~/Repositories/slidev-village
- [ ] ~20 slides covering all planned sections
- [ ] Custom dark theme applied
- [ ] Mermaid diagrams for handoff chain and permission matrix
- [ ] Placeholder components for screenshots/videos to be filled later
- [ ] QR code placeholders for GitHub links

## Notes

Slide order: Title → Problem → Village Model → Five Agents → Beads → Commands & Workflow → Success Story → Drawbacks → Links/QR

title: Initialise Slidev project and custom dark theme
description:
## Context

Scaffold the Slidev project at ~/Repositories/slidev-village with proper package.json, install @slidev/cli and dependencies, and create a custom CSS theme based on the AstroNvim Vivid palette.

Theme colors (from ~/.config/opencode/themes/astronvim-vivid.json):
- Background: #000000
- Text: #ADB0BB, Muted: #595C66, Comment: #696C76
- Primary/Blue: #57b6ff
- Secondary/Purple: #eba0ff
- Accent/Cyan: #5efff5
- Success/Green: #b0ff7f
- Warning/Orange: #ffa942
- Error/Red: #ff8191
- Yellow: #ffcf32

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/slidev-village`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] package.json with @slidev/cli, dev/build/export scripts
- [ ] npm install runs cleanly
- [ ] style.css with CSS custom properties mapping AstroNvim Vivid palette
- [ ] slidev frontmatter in slides.md configured for dark theme
- [ ] `npm run dev` starts the presentation without errors
- [ ] Create a Placeholder.vue component in components/ that renders a styled box with a label (for screenshot placeholders)

## Notes

Use Slidev's CSS variable override system. Keep the theme minimal — dark bg, vibrant accent colors for headings/code/links.
close_reason: Checks passed: build (npm run build) PASS, dev server starts cleanly on :3030. Lint/typecheck/test skipped (no scripts configured). All AC met per inspector judgment.

title: Title + Problem slides (slides 1-3)
description:
## Context

Create the opening three slides of the presentation:

**Slide 1 — Title:**
- Title: "OpenCode Village — Orchestrating AI Agents for Software Development"
- Subtitle/tagline: Role-separated, bead-tracked, skill-driven AI workflow
- Author name, date

**Slide 2 — The Problem (part 1):**
- Single-agent AI lacks structure
- No accountability — it can edit, test, push all at once
- Context is lost between sessions (compaction kills memory)
- No audit trail of decisions
- Hard to reason about what the AI did and why

**Slide 3 — The Problem (part 2) / Transition:**
- "What if your AI assistant had coworkers?"
- Transition to the village metaphor
- Brief visual/text hook leading into the village model

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/slidev-village`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Title slide with presentation name, tagline, author placeholder
- [ ] Problem slide(s) articulating single-agent limitations (4-5 bullet points)
- [ ] Transition hook slide leading into the village model
- [ ] Slides use the custom dark theme from bd-139.1

## Notes

Keep text concise — this is a talk, not a doc. Use speaker notes for elaboration.
close_reason: Checks passed: build (npm run build) succeeded — 421 modules, 1.73s. No lint/typecheck/test scripts configured. Slides 1-3 verified: Title, Problem, and Transition slides present with correct theme tokens.

title: Village Model overview + Permission Matrix slides (slides 4-5)
description:
## Context

Create two slides introducing the village model architecture:

**Slide 4 — The Village Model:**
- Mermaid flowchart showing the handoff pipeline: mayor → worker → inspector → guard → (envoy)
- Include feedback loops: inspector → worker (changes requested), guard → worker (checks failed), inspector → mayor (out of scope)
- Key idea: separation of concerns via permissions — just like a real team

**Slide 5 — Permission Matrix:**
- HTML or markdown table showing what each agent can/can't do:

| Capability | Mayor | Worker | Inspector | Guard | Envoy |
|-----------|-------|--------|-----------|-------|-------|
| Read code | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Edit files | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Local commits | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Run tests/lint | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Git push | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Create beads | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| Open PRs | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/slidev-village`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Mermaid diagram of the handoff pipeline with feedback loops renders correctly
- [ ] Permission matrix table is visually clean and readable
- [ ] Both slides use the custom dark theme

## Notes

Use Slidev's built-in Mermaid support. The diagram is the visual centrepiece of the talk — make it clear and uncluttered. Consider using colours from the theme palette for different agents in the diagram.

comments.author: technoch1ef
comments.text: Note: The slides.md file still contains the default Slidev template. The work from bd-139.1 (custom dark theme) and bd-139.2 (slides 1-3) appears to not be committed to the file. Proceeding to add slides 4-5 at the end of the file.

comments.author: technoch1ef
comments.text:
Implementation complete. Handing off to inspector for review.

## Handoff Summary
- Goal: Create slides 4-5 (Village Model + Permission Matrix) for the Slidev presentation
- Current status: Both slides implemented and committed on epic/slidev-village
- What changed: Users/oleksandrovcharov/Repositories/slidev-village/slides.md — added 2 slides at end of file

## What was implemented

### Slide 4 — The Village Model
- Mermaid LR flowchart with 5 agent nodes (Mayor, Worker, Inspector, Guard, Envoy)
- Forward pipeline: Mayor→Worker→Inspector→Guard→Envoy
- 3 feedback loops: Inspector→Worker (changes requested), Guard→Worker (checks failed), Inspector→Mayor (out of scope)
- Color-coded nodes using classDef (purple/blue/amber/green/red)
- Presenter notes explaining the design rationale

### Slide 5 — Permission Matrix
- 7-row table with all capabilities from the AC (read code, edit files, local commits, run tests/lint, git push, create beads, open PRs)
- Color-coded agent name headers using UnoCSS text- classes
- Tagline: 'Permissions are enforced by agent system prompts — not trust, but design.'
- Presenter notes

## Known Issues
- slides.md still contains the default Slidev template (bd-139.1 theme and bd-139.2 slides 1-3 work appears uncommitted)
- The new slides are appended at the end, before the 'Learn More' closer
- Custom dark theme from bd-139.1 is not present; slides use inline colours that work on both light and dark backgrounds

## How to verify
- npx slidev slides.md (in the slidev-village directory) to preview
- Check Mermaid diagram renders the pipeline with all 3 feedback loops
- Check permission matrix table has all 7 capability rows with correct checkmarks

comments.author: technoch1ef
comments.text:
**Inspector judgment for bead bd-139.3**

### AC coverage
- [ ] Mermaid diagram of the handoff pipeline with feedback loops renders correctly — PARTIALLY: The Mermaid content is correct (5 agent nodes, forward pipeline Mayor→Worker→Inspector→Guard→Envoy, 3 feedback loops: Inspector→Worker changes requested, Guard→Worker checks failed, Inspector→Mayor out of scope, color-coded classDef). However, the diagram was committed to the WRONG REPOSITORY (opencode-village instead of slidev-village) and embedded in the default Slidev template, so it will not render in the actual presentation.
- [ ] Permission matrix table is visually clean and readable — PARTIALLY: Table has all 7 capability rows with correct checkmarks/crosses and color-coded headers. However, it uses UnoCSS classes (text-purple-400 etc.) instead of var(--vivid-*) CSS custom properties used by slides 1-3.
- [ ] Both slides use the custom dark theme — NOT SATISFIED: The committed file uses theme: seriph (default Slidev template) with frontmatter title 'Welcome to Slidev'. The custom dark theme from bd-139.1 (colorSchema: dark, var(--vivid-*) tokens) is not referenced. Worker acknowledged this in handoff notes.

### Diff scope
- FLAG: File committed to WRONG REPOSITORY. The worker committed slides.md to the opencode-village repo at relative path Users/oleksandrovcharov/Repositories/slidev-village/slides.md (703 lines). The actual Slidev project is a separate git repo at /Users/oleksandrovcharov/Repositories/slidev-village/ where bd-139.1 and bd-139.2 were correctly committed.
- FLAG: 703-line file is the ENTIRE default Slidev template with slides 4-5 appended near the end. Only ~50 lines are actual new work; the other ~650 lines are the default starter template (Welcome to Slidev, What is Slidev?, Navigation, Markdown, Code examples, etc.)
- FLAG: The actual slidev-village repo's slides.md (104 lines, correctly themed slides 1-3) does NOT contain slides 4-5.

### Regression sniff
- FLAG: The commit pollutes the opencode-village repo with an absolute-path-as-relative-path file from a separate project. Git status also shows 9 untracked files from the slidev-village project leaking into opencode-village's working tree.
- OK: No TODO/FIXME/console.log/hardcoded secrets in the slide content itself.

### Stack review (stack-typescript)
- N/A: Changes are Slidev Markdown, not TypeScript. No TS code involved.

**Verdict: changes requested**

### Required fixes:
1. **Commit to the correct repository**: The slides must be committed in the slidev-village repo at /Users/oleksandrovcharov/Repositories/slidev-village/, not in the opencode-village repo.
2. **Append to existing slides.md**: The slidev-village repo already has slides.md with slides 1-3 (104 lines, ending with a placeholder comment). Slides 4-5 should be appended there, replacing the placeholder comment. Do NOT include the default Slidev template.
3. **Use custom dark theme tokens**: Use var(--vivid-purple), var(--vivid-cyan), var(--vivid-orange) etc. for agent colours in the permission matrix headers, consistent with slides 1-3. The Mermaid classDef inline colours are acceptable (Mermaid does not support CSS custom properties).
4. **Clean up opencode-village repo**: Remove the accidentally committed file at Users/oleksandrovcharov/Repositories/slidev-village/slides.md from the opencode-village repo.

title: Five Agents deep-dive slides (slides 6-10)
description:
## Context

Create five slides, one per agent, with a consistent layout:

**Slide 6 — Mayor (Planner):**
- Plans, researches, creates epics + child beads
- Read-only — never modifies files
- Every bead gets: Context, Skills, Branch, Acceptance Criteria
- Creates beads immediately (no waiting for approval)
- [Placeholder: screenshot of mayor creating beads in OpenCode]

**Slide 7 — Worker (Implementer):**
- Implements exactly what a bead asks for
- Claims work via village_claim (single in-progress enforced)
- Commits locally on epic/* branches
- Loads skills from the bead's Skills section
- Hands off to inspector when done
- [Placeholder: screenshot of worker implementing]

**Slide 8 — Inspector (Reviewer):**
- Read-only code review — cannot edit any files
- Judgment checklist: AC coverage, diff scope, regression sniff
- Three verdicts: Approve → guard, Changes requested → worker, Out of scope → mayor
- Catches issues before expensive CI runs
- [Placeholder: screenshot of inspector review]

**Slide 9 — Guard (Verifier):**
- Runs mechanical check matrix: lint, typecheck, test, build
- Polymorphic via stack skills (same guard, different checks)
- All green → closes bead; any red → returns to worker
- Cascade-closes parent epic when all children done
- [Placeholder: screenshot of guard running checks]

**Slide 10 — Envoy (Diplomat):**
- Optional, human-triggered only (/village:envoy)
- Pushes branches, opens GitHub PRs
- Cannot force push or create branches
- Minimal PR template: What, Why, Closes
- [Placeholder: screenshot of PR created by envoy]

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/slidev-village`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Five slides, one per agent, with consistent visual layout
- [ ] Each slide has: agent name/role, 4-5 key bullet points, a Placeholder component for screenshots
- [ ] Visual distinction between agents (consider using different accent colours from the theme palette)
- [ ] Speaker notes with additional talking points

## Notes

Use the Placeholder.vue component from bd-139.1 for screenshot placeholders. Keep bullet points short — the speaker will elaborate.

title: Beads + Context Preservation slides (slides 11-13)
description:
## Context

Create three slides about the beads issue tracking system:

**Slide 11 — What Are Beads?**
- Git-native, AI-first issue tracker — issues live inside the repo in .beads/
- SQLite locally, synced to JSONL for git versioning
- Issue types: task, bug, feature, epic, chore, decision, question, docs
- Priority: P0 (critical) → P4 (backlog)
- Dependencies: blocks, parent-child, discovered-from
- No external API needed — issues travel with the code
- Show a few br CLI examples: br create, br show, br ready, br close

**Slide 12 — Context Preservation (br prime):**
- AI sessions have limited context windows — compaction kills memory
- br prime outputs AI-optimised summary of all beads state
- The opencode-beads-rust plugin auto-injects on session start AND after compaction
- This is the "memory" that ties agents across sessions together
- Discovery chains: agent finds bug while working on feature → links with discovered-from → future agents see the full context

**Slide 13 — beads_viewer (bv):**
- TUI for browsing issues interactively
- [Placeholder: screenshot/video of beads_viewer showing a board with issues]
- [Placeholder: screenshot of dependency graph]
- Mention: agents use br CLI with --json, humans use bv

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/slidev-village`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Slide explaining what beads are with key bullet points and CLI examples
- [ ] Slide on context preservation with br prime explanation
- [ ] Slide for beads_viewer with Placeholder components for TUI screenshots
- [ ] Code blocks with br CLI command examples styled with theme colours

## Notes

The br prime / context preservation slide is crucial — it answers "how do different AI sessions share knowledge?" Make it visually impactful.

title: Commands + Workflow walkthrough slides (slides 14-15)
description:
## Context

Create two slides covering the slash commands and a workflow walkthrough:

**Slide 14 — Slash Commands:**
- /village:work — The main work loop. Claim → load skills → implement → handoff → repeat
- /village:board — ASCII board of current village state (who has what bead, what status)
- /village:orphans [fix] — Find unassigned beads, optionally auto-assign
- /village:envoy <id> — Dispatch envoy to push branch and open PR
- Show brief code/CLI examples of each

**Slide 15 — Workflow Walkthrough:**
- A step-by-step visual of a typical cycle:
  1. User runs /village:work as mayor
  2. Mayor creates epic + 3 child beads
  3. User switches to worker, runs /village:work
  4. Worker claims bead, implements, commits, hands off to inspector
  5. Inspector reviews diff, approves → guard
  6. Guard runs tests, all green → closes bead
  7. Repeat for remaining beads
  8. User runs /village:envoy → PR opened
- Consider a Mermaid sequence diagram or a numbered list with icons

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/slidev-village`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Slide listing all 4 slash commands with brief descriptions
- [ ] Workflow walkthrough slide showing a complete cycle end-to-end
- [ ] CLI/code examples for command usage
- [ ] Visual flow (Mermaid sequence diagram or styled numbered list)

## Notes

The walkthrough slide should tell a story — the audience should be able to follow a complete feature from idea to PR.

title: Success Story slides (slides 16-17)
description:
## Context

Create two slides showcasing the real-world success story:

**Slide 16 — Success: Vibecoding Go Without Knowing Go:**
- Feature: "Omni in Slack" (formerly Ask Outreach) — AI assistant integration in Slack
- Stats callout (make these visually prominent):
  - 22 merged PRs
  - 3 Go repositories (notifications, notificationswebhook, hephaestus)
  - ~4 weeks
  - Zero prior Go knowledge
- Types of work delivered:
  - Streaming AI delivery pipeline
  - Slack Block Kit conversion from protobuf
  - Citation resolver (deep links to Outreach app)
  - Multi-org switching with Slack modals
  - Identity store race condition fixes
  - Full rebrand (Ask Outreach → Omni)

**Slide 17 — Why the Village Helped:**
- Mayor decomposed a complex multi-repo epic into small, reviewable beads
- Worker handled Go idioms — the AI knows Go even if you don't
- Inspector caught scope creep and regressions before CI
- Guard ran all Go tests (860+ per package) mechanically
- Structured handoffs prevented "yolo shipping" to production
- Beads preserved context across dozens of sessions

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/slidev-village`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Slide with eye-catching stats (22 PRs, 3 repos, 4 weeks, zero Go)
- [ ] Bullet points on types of work delivered
- [ ] Slide explaining why the village model specifically enabled this
- [ ] Stats should be visually prominent (large numbers, accent colours)

## Notes

This is the "proof it works" section. The stats should be the first thing the audience sees. Consider a grid/card layout for the numbers.

title: Drawbacks slide (slide 18)
description:
## Context

Create one honest slide about the drawbacks:

**Slide 18 — Drawbacks & Honest Takes:**

Token Usage:
- Multiple agent sessions = multiple context windows
- Inspector + guard review burns tokens even on obvious code
- Rough estimate: ~3-5x more tokens than single-agent for same output
- Each handoff reloads context (beads, skills, diff)

Speed:
- Sequential handoff pipeline is inherently slower
- Worker → inspector → guard can take 10-15 minutes per bead
- Single-agent can do the same in 2-5 minutes (but with less quality assurance)

The Tradeoff:
- Quality + auditability + accountability vs. speed + cost
- Worth it for complex features (multi-repo, unfamiliar languages)
- Overkill for one-liner fixes
- The workflow is configurable — you can skip inspector/guard for trivial changes

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/slidev-village`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Single slide covering token usage, speed, and the tradeoff
- [ ] Honest, balanced tone — not defensive
- [ ] Visual comparison or callout boxes for the key numbers

## Notes

Being upfront about limitations builds credibility. Frame it as a conscious tradeoff, not a bug.

title: Links, QR codes + closing slide (slides 19-20)
description:
## Context

  - technoch1ef/opencode-beads-rust — the beads OpenCode integration

  - opencode-ai/opencode — the OpenCode project itself
- npm packages:
  - @technoch1ef/opencode-village
  - @technoch1ef/opencode-beads-rust

- [Placeholder: QR code for opencode-beads-rust repo]

## Notes

title: Epic: Remove all Solana references from codebase
description:
## Context

Remove all mentions of Solana, Anchor, and the stack-solana skill from the opencode-village codebase. This includes detection logic, skill assets, agent permissions, test fixtures, and documentation.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] No grep hits for solana, Solana, stack-solana, Anchor.toml, anchor-lang, anchor build, anchor test (excluding CHANGELOG historical entries)
- [ ] All tests pass after removal
- [ ] Build succeeds

## Notes

CHANGELOG.md entries are historical records. They may be lightly edited for accuracy but should not be fully purged — just updated so they do not imply Solana support still exists.

title: Delete stack-solana skill asset and remove Solana detection logic
description:

title: Update tests and fixtures for Solana removal
description:
## Context

Remove Solana-specific tests, fixtures, and update tests that reference stack-solana.

## Skills

- beads-workflow
- stack-typescript

## Branch

`epic/remove-solana`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Delete test fixture directories: `tests/fixtures/detect-stack/solana-anchor/` and `tests/fixtures/detect-stack/solana-programs/`
- [ ] Update `tests/fixtures/detect-stack/multi-stack/`: remove `Anchor.toml` from it (this fixture should only test TypeScript after Solana removal)
- [ ] In `tests/detect-stack.test.ts`: remove the two `detects stack-solana` test cases (lines 19-29)
- [ ] In `tests/detect-stack.test.ts`: update the `detects multiple stacks in multi-stack project` test (line 42-47) — remove the `expect(skills).toContain("stack-solana")` assertion
- [ ] In `tests/detect-stack.test.ts`: update the `mergeSkills` test (lines 99-108) — remove `stack-solana` from inputs and expected outputs
- [ ] In `tests/detect-stack.test.ts`: update the second `mergeSkills` test (line 112) — replace `stack-solana` with another skill (e.g. `stack-ruby-on-rails`)
- [ ] In `tests/detect-stack.test.ts`: update the `injectSkillsIntoBody` test (lines 196-203) — replace `stack-solana` with another skill (e.g. `stack-ruby-on-rails`)
- [ ] No remaining references to `stack-solana`, `solana-anchor`, or `solana-programs` in `tests/`

## Notes

The multi-stack fixture after this change will just be a TypeScript project (has package.json). The multi-stack tests for `detects multiple stacks` should be reworked to detect TypeScript + Rails or similar, or simplified to just verify TypeScript detection from that fixture.
